coredata.dc:title: Bilayer pH-sensitive colorimetric indicator films based on chitosan/purple carrot extract and gellan gum/Mg-carbon dots for visual monitoring of pork freshness

coredata.dc:description: A pH-sensitive colorimetric bilayer film was constructed on the basis of layer-by-layer self-assembly, with chitosan (CS)-loaded purple carrot anthocyanin (PCA) as the inner indicator layer and gellan gum (GG)-loaded Mg-doped carbon dots (Mg-CDs) as the protective outer layer. X-ray diffraction and scanning electron microscopy analyses confirmed the successful preparation of the bilayer film and the incorporation of Mg-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy indicated that the Mg-CDs formed hydrogen bonds with the film matrix. Thermogravimetric analysis revealed that the Mg-CDs improved the thermal stability of the double-layer films. Moreover, the addition of 7 wt% Mg-CDs increased the water vapor permeability, oxygen permeability, and tensile strength of the films by 0.94-, 0.79-, and 3.18-fold, respectively. The antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the films were significantly enhanced by including Mg-CDs as the active agents. Illumination experiments revealed that the relative color change (S values) increased by only 2.76 % and 4.69 % after the addition of 7 wt% Mg-CDs to the GG -Mg-CDs layer after 21 days of visible light and 72 h of UV light exposure, respectively. These bilayer films containing PCA and Mg-CDs exhibit good pH color responsiveness and biosafety. The C-P/G-7 %CD film had the best overall performance and can effectively reflect pork freshness during storage. Therefore, this research has great potential for developing a double-layer film for monitoring meat quality changes in intelligent food packaging.

head.ce:title: Bilayer pH-sensitive colorimetric indicator films based on chitosan/purple carrot extract and gellan gum/Mg-carbon dots for visual monitoring of pork freshness

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pH-sensitive colorimetric bilayer film was constructed on the basis of layer-by-layer self-assembly, with chitosan (CS)-loaded purple carrot anthocyanin (PCA) as the inner indicator layer and gellan gum (GG)-loaded Mg-doped carbon dots (Mg-CDs) as the protective outer layer. X-ray diffraction and scanning electron microscopy analyses confirmed the successful preparation of the bilayer film and the incorporation of Mg-CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy indicated that the Mg-CDs formed hydrogen bonds with the film matrix. Thermogravimetric analysis revealed that the Mg-CDs improved the thermal stability of the double-layer films. Moreover, the addition of 7 wt% Mg-CDs increased the water vapor permeability, oxygen permeability, and tensile strength of the films by 0.94-, 0.79-, and 3.18-fold, respectively. The antioxidant and antimicrobial activities of the films were significantly enhanced by including Mg-CDs as the active agents. Illumination experiments revealed that the relative color change (S values) increased by only 2.76 % and 4.69 % after the addition of 7 wt% Mg-CDs to the GG -Mg-CDs layer after 21 days of visible light and 72 h of UV light exposure, respectively. These bilayer films containing PCA and Mg-CDs exhibit good pH color responsiveness and biosafety. The C-P/G-7 %CD film had the best overall performance and can effectively reflect pork freshness during storage. Therefore, this research has great potential for developing a double-layer film for monitoring meat quality changes in intelligent food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Meat is susceptible to spoilage due to microbial contamination and enzymatic reactions. Generally, dimethylamine, trimethylamine, ammonia and other volatile nitrogen compounds are generated, and the increase in the total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content alters the pH of the meat in the packaging environment (Huang et al., 2023b). Hence, the development of a visual pH index to indicate food freshness has received widespread attention. Many reports have proven that anthocyanins in plants such as purple potatoes, roselles, purple kale, and mulberries are sensitive to pH (Wu & Li, 2023) and can serve as safe natural indicators for monitoring the freshness of meat. Therefore, anthocyanins have been used in pH-sensitive colorimetric films because of their nontoxicity and pH sensitivity (Yang et al. 2021).

Chitosan (CS) is a linear cationic polysaccharide obtained by deacetylating chitin (Cao et al. 2023). Chitosan has been widely applied in food packaging because of its excellent ability to form films, inherent antibacterial activity, and good biocompatibility (Zhang et al., 2022). Recently, scholars have invented a new generation of biodegradable CS-based packaging materials. For example, Xie et al. (2023) invented a chitosan/chitin nanofiber film that was combined with scallion flower extract for fresh-cut banana preservation. Wang et al. (2023b) invented a chitosan/fucoidan film containing coleus grass leaf anthocyanins to monitor salmon spoilage. However, the potential of CS films in food packaging is restricted because of their poor barrier properties and antioxidant capacity (Wang et al. 2023c).

To enhance the performance of CS-based films, especially their thermal stability, mechanical performance and barrier properties, CS can be blended with other polymers. For example, a mixture of CS and gelatin can participate in certain interactions by forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds between polymer chains, creating a dense three-dimensional network structure and enhancing the mechanical and physicochemical properties of the composite film (Khan et al., 2023). However, the anthocyanin-based colorimetric film developed by blending polymers as a matrix could not improve the stability of internal anthocyanins to factors such as ultraviolet light, temperature, and oxygen. Among them, ultraviolet irradiation is a crucial factor influencing the color stability of anthocyanins in biopolymerized films. Moreover, research has shown that ultraviolet light strongly affects the stability of anthocyanins exposed to high temperatures. Therefore, improving the light resistance of anthocyanin-based films is the key to realizing their long-term stability (Zhai et al., 2020).

To enhance the mechanical and barrier performance and light stability of polymer films, many scholars have created double-layer films, in which the outer film containing functional nanomaterials is used to protect the inner film containing anthocyanin. For example, Yang et al. (2022) developed an agar-based bilayer active film, in which TiO2 nanoparticles were added to the outer layer to effectively protect red radish anthocyanins and neem essential oils in the inner layer. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from biowaste have been widely utilized as active biopolymeric film fillers to increase their functionality. CDs are a new type of nanomaterial with particle sizes less than 10 nm. They have unique properties, such as antibacterial and antioxidant effects, chemical stability, and UV blocking ability (Khan et al., 2024). However, some studies have shown that the antibacterial activity of CDs relies on photodynamics; that is, photosensitizer activation by visible light, triggers biochemical reactions to inactivate microorganisms (Chen et al., 2023). Moreover, in the absence of a suitable stabilizer, the formed nanoparticles tend to aggregate, resulting in reduced antimicrobial and antioxidant effects. In this regard, stable and functional CDs can be produced by doping with metal nanoparticles (Koshy et al., 2022). Therefore, doping MgO nanoparticles (Mg-NPs) into CDs is an excellent strategy for obtaining stable CDs with excellent functional properties. The CDs chemical structure consists of central carbon nuclei and surface functional groups. The physicochemical features of CDs may vary depending on the chemical structure of the CDs and the specific functional moieties present on the carbon source. Upon doping, the OH and COOH groups on the CDs surface bind to metal nanoparticles to stabilize the final structure (Riahi et al., 2024). Here, we hypothesize that adding Mg-doped CDs (Mg-CDs) to the protective outer layer of a double-layer film can effectively improve the physical and functional properties and stability of the resulting polymer film.

Purple carrots are rich in anthocyanins, and purple carrot extract has strong pH sensitivity, making it a good candidate active filler for intelligent active packaging. Many scientists have synthesized CDs from the residue that remains after the active ingredient has been extracted, such as banana peels (Sul et al., 2023) and Brassica oleracea waste (Wagh et al., 2023). Riahi et al. (2024) prepared carrageenan-based films using anthocyanins extracted from sweet potato peels and biowaste derived carbon dots for the real-time monitoring of fresh shrimp freshness in an active packaging experiment. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no research on the use of purple carrot anthocyanins as pH-sensitive active inner film fillers and biowaste-derived Mg-CDs as the enhancing outer film phase for the development of intelligent active packaging.

Hence, in this study, we aimed to develop a bilayer film with improved photostability and functional properties to monitor meat spoilage. The inner layer of the bilayer film was a chitosan-anthocyanin (CS-PCA) blend, which served as the sensing layer. Chitosan is a linear cationic polysaccharide with excellent film-forming ability and good biocompatibility. The outer layer of the bilayer was a gellan gum-Mg-CDs (GG-Mg-CDs) blend that served as a protective layer. The Mg-CDs have excellent chemical stability and UV-blocking ability. Therefore, Mg-CDs were utilized to protect the anthocyanins in the photographic layer from UV irradiation, improving the illumination stability of the anthocyanins. Gellan gum is negatively charged, which allows a polyelectrolyte composite film to form in the presence of an oppositely charged polymer (CS), potentially further improving the film properties. In addition, the intermolecular interactions, microstructure and physicochemical properties of the films, as well as the effects of different concentrations of Mg-CDs on the mechanical properties, barrier properties, pH response and light stability, were studied.
The materials are described in Section S.1. (Supplementary Information).

The extraction of anthocyanins from purple carrots was performed according to the methodology of Xie et al. (2023), with slight modification. First, the purple carrots were washed, cut into small pieces, freeze-dried and ground into powder. The anthocyanins in purple carrots were subsequently extracted by soaking 10 g of purple carrot powder in 100 mL of 80% ethanol (v/v) supplemented with 1% HCl (v/v) at 4 °C for 24 h and stirring every 2 h. The extraction mixture was subjected to 10 min of centrifugation at 5000 rpm, after which the extraction mixture was filtered through qualitative filter paper and the solvent was evaporated with a rotary evaporator. Finally, the anthocyanins were freeze-dried in the dark and stored at −20 °C. The anthocyanins, herein called PCA, were obtained from purple carrots. PCA was characterized according to the methods in the Supporting Information.

After PCA was extraction, the purple carrot waste residue was dried at 50 °C for use as a carbon precursor for CDs synthesis. Mg-CDs were synthesized according to the methods of Khan et al. (2024). The purple carrot peel (1 g) and 50 mg of MgO nanoparticles (5 wt% purple carrot powder) were dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water and stirred for 10 min, after which the mixed solution was put into a reactor and heated at 190 °C for 6 h. Later, the solution was cooled to room temperature (25 °C) to obtain a yellow brown solution, and centrifugation was carried out at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane filter (0.22 µm pore size, 25 mm diameter nylon filter) to obtain a brownyellow Mg-CDs, which was stored at 4 °C for further analyses.

To prepare the GG solution, 2 g of GG powder was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water and stirred magnetically in a water bath at 95 °C for 1 h. Next, different mass fractions of Mg-CDs (1%−7% on basis of the mass of GG) were added to the GG solution with vigorous stirring for 30 min (Chen et al., 2023). The GG-Mg-CDs mixture was sonicated for 5 min, after which 15 g of GG-Mg-CDs mixture was immediately poured into a 9 cm diameter plastic Petri dish. After cooling, this mixture formed the upper layer of double-layer hydrogel.

2 g of CS powder was dissolved in 100 mL of 2% aqueous acetic acid and stirred at 85 °C for 1 h. Afterward, 0.12 g of anthocyanin powder (6%, on the basis of CS) was added at 35 °C to prepare an anthocyanin solution (Wagh et al., 2023). The CS-PCA solution was degassed, and 15 g of this solution was immediately poured over the GG-Mg-CDs layer of the hydrogel. After cooling, the CS-PCA solution formed the lower layer of the double-layer hydrogel.

Moreover, a CS-PCA/GG film without Mg-CDs was generated as a control and was named C-P/GG. All of the double-layer hydrogels were dried at 40 °C for 4 h to allow film formation and stored at 75% relative humidity for no less than 48 h. The bilayer films with different amounts of Mg-CDs (1%, 3%, 5% and 7%) were named C-P/G-1%CD, C-P/G-3%CD, C-P/G-5%CD and C-P/G-7%CD, respectively. The preparation process of bilayer films was illustrated in Scheme 1.
The physicochemical characterization methods for the as-prepared Mg-CDs and films are described in Supporting Information.

In our experiments, the film containing 7 wt% Mg-CDs was found to have the best mechanical, light stability, pH sensitivity, antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Therefore, C-P/G-7%CD films were used to monitor pork freshness. Fresh pork samples were placed in Petri dishes (9 cm) and a C-P/G-7%CD film (1 × 1 cm) was fixed on top of the dishes before storage at room temperature or 4 °C. The pH value of pork was determined according to the method of Wang et al. (2023a), with slight modifications. Minced pork (2 g) was mixed with 20 mL of deionized water, and the solution pH was measured with a pH meter (100pro, Lichen, China). The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content in the pork sample was determined with an automatic Kjeldahl analyzer (KN520, ALVA, China).

All tests were repeated at least three times, and the data are presented as the means ± standard deviations. Statistical software for the Social Sciences (SPSS) 25.0 was used to analyze differences between the means of the two groups using Duncan's multiple comparison test; a value of p < 0.05 was considered to indicate statistical significance.

The total phenolic content and total anthocyanin content in PCA were 585 ± 2.47 mg/g and 70.40 ± 4.28 mg/g, respectively. The PCA DPPH and ABTS scavenging rates were 90.10 ± 0.74% and 94.21 ± 0.86%, respectively, which was primarily due to the high polyphenolic content in PCA. Fig. 1a shows the changes in the color of PCA in buffer solutions with pH values ranging from 3.0 to 11.0. Research has shown that the anthocyanin structure changes in different acidic or alkaline environments, which causes them exhibit different color changes (Chen et al., 2022). The reddish coloration of PCA at pH 3.0 occurred because of the formation of flavonoid cations under acidic conditions, and as the pH of the solution increased from 4.0 to 6.0, the solution changed to faint pink or reddish-purple color owing to the formation of methanol pseudobases and chalcones (Wang et al., 2023b). The purplish color at pH 7.0, the blue color at pH 8.0 and the dark blue color at pH 9.0 resulted from the conversion of anthocyanins to alkaline quinones (Wang et al., 2023b). A brown color was observed at pH 10.0, and a yellow color was observed at pH 11.0 because of the transformation of the chalcone structure (Oladzadabbasabadi et al., 2022). Fig. 1a also shows the UVvis absorption spectra of anthocyanins in buffer solutions with diverse pH values (from 3.0 to 11.0). As the pH changed, the UVvisible absorbance peak of the PCA solution notably shifted. When the pH of the PCA solution was ≤ 7, the absorption peak was observed at 520–540 nm, and when the pH of the PCA solution was > 7, the maximum absorption peak was found at 580–600 nm. These shifts occurred because of the color change of the anthocyanins. Huang et al. (2023a) reported a comparable shift in the UVvis absorption peak of cauliflower extract from 525 to 615 nm under different pH conditions (from 2.0 to 13.0). The components in the extract were further characterized by HPLC-MS (Table S2 in Supplementary Information). The results showed that the major polyphenolic compounds in PCA were anthocyanins. In addition, PCA also contains a variety of polyphenolic compounds.

The microstructure and morphology of the prepared Mg-CDs recorded by TEM are shown in Fig. 1b. Mg-CDs were monodispersed in different shapes and were all smaller than 10 nm in quantum range. Fig. 1c displays the particle size profile of the Mg-CDs, which ranged from 0.6 to 2.2 nm. The optical properties of the Mg-CDs were determined by UVvis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. UVvis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1d, black line) revealed similar light absorption patterns and absorption peaks to those of the CDs synthesized by Roy et al. (2021). There was a strong absorption band near 300 nm that can result from n-π * transitions (C-OH or CO bonds). The Mg–CD emitted blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (illustration, Fig. 1d). This transient color variation under UV light is unique to CDs, which confirmed their successful synthesis. The fluorescence features of the Mg-CDs were determined by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1e shows the excitation-dependent fluorescence emission of the Mg-CDs. When the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 440 nm, an obvious and consistent shift of the color of the emission peak was observed. The probable cause of such coordinated emission is surface defects, which create tunable emissions with distinct energy states (Roy et al., 2021). The Mg-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescence peak ranging from 350–650 nm at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, with a maximum intensity at approximately 458 nm. The fluorescence emission of CDs is tunable because the carbon source, synthesis pathway, size and shape of CDs differ, as do the fluorescence parameters, such as emission sites and functional groups on the CD surface (Khan et al., 2023).

The FTIR spectrum of the Mg-CDs exhibited a broad band at 3200–3500 cm−1, corresponding to O-H/N-H group stretching (Fig. 1f). The peaks near 2933 and 1210 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C-H and -CH2 bonds present in methyl and methylene groups, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1632, 1427, and 1067 cm−1 resulted from CO, C-N, and C-O-C bond vibrations, respectively. In Fig. 1f, the doped MgO reacts with the CO groups on the surface of the CDs, decreasing the CO content in the CDs (Khan et al., 2024). CO groups are critical for CDs passivation; therefore, the observed reduction in the intensity of the characteristic CO peak verified the interaction between the metal and the CDs surface. Furthermore, in the FTIR spectrum of the Mg-CDs, the peak at 3420 cm−1 broadened, and the peak positions from the participating groups shifted slightly, which suggests that the intermolecular hydrogen bonds formed between the OH groups of the CDs and MgO also affects Mg-CDs formation (Khan et al., 2024).

XPS analysis was used to confirm the structure and presence of the constituent elements and the chemical composition of the Mg-CDs (Fig. 1g). The four characteristic peaks at 284.5, 398.9, 530.6 and 1309.8 eV indicate the presence of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Mg 1 s in the Mg-CDs, respectively. Moreover, the full spectrum showed that the Mg-CDs contained C (68.64 ± 0.47%), O (26.19 ± 0.36%), N (4.10 ± 0.42%) and Mg (1.08 ± 0.17%). The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1h) was decomposed into three peaks at 289.8, 286.3 and 284.7 eV, corresponding to CO, C-O/C-N and CC/C-C, respectively. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1i) showed peaks at 402.0, 400.2 and 399.1 eV, which were attributed to N-H, N-(C)3, and C-N-C bonds, respectively (Zhao et al., 2015). Fig. 1j shows the fragmentation of the high-resolution XPS O 1 s spectrum into four peaks at 530.2, 530.7, 534.4 and 533.5 eV, which were assigned to metal-O, C-O, CO and C-O-H groups, respectively (Li et al., 2015). Furthermore, deconvolution of the XPS Mg 1 s spectrum displayed one component peak located at 1304.3 eV, which was attributed to Mg-O (Fig. 1k). Fig. 1l shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized Mg-CDs, with a wide peak at 2θ = 22.98°. Moreover, the Mg-CDs and MgO have completely different XRD patterns. The broad peak suggests that the Mg-CDs are disordered. These observations are consistent with the previously reported biocompatible carbon quanta extracted from industrial sugarcane waste (Pandiyan et al., 2020).

The most common way to analyze the antioxidant activity of substances is to assess their ability to neutralize or scavenge free radicals (Khan et al., 2024). The antioxidant activities of both the Mg-CDs and CDs increased in a concentration-dependent manner. At 12.5 μg/mL, the antioxidant capacity of the CDs toward DPPH was 15.87 ± 0.16%, and that of the Mg-CDs toward DPPH was 28.76 ± 0.78% (Fig. S1a in Supplementary Information). Moreover, the antioxidant activity of the CDs toward ABTS was 17.40 ± 0.91%, whereas the antioxidant capacity of the Mg-CDs toward ABTS was 29.17 ± 0.78% (Fig. S1b in Supplementary Information). As the concentration of Mg-CDs increased to 100 μg/mL, the ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging capacities were 98.63 ± 0.45% and 93.72 ± 0.63%, respectively. These CDs may be able to scavenge radicals due to the delocalization of free electrons and stabilization of the sp2 carbon structure, including the hydrogen-donating behavior of edge functional groups such as COOH, NH2 and OH (Das et al., 2023). DPPH and ABTS contain one N atom and a lone pair of electrons surrounded by three benzene rings that undergo a hydrogen shift and react with Mg-CDs to form the complexes ABTS-H and DPPH-H. The reduction and quenching of ABTS and DPPH radicals are able to be achieved by hydrogen transfer of hydroxyl functional groups of CDs. Additionally, the anti-free radical activity of the CDs is facilitated by electron donation, which results in the formation of adducts. Adding metal dopants can amplify the electron and proton transfer abilities of CDs and enhance their antioxidant effects (Riahi et al., 2024).

The antibacterial activity of CDs and Mg-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus was demonstrated by agar well-diffusion assays (Fig. S1c-d in Supplementary Information). The zones of inhibition produced by the CDs with S. aureus and E. coli were 13.29 ± 0.44 mm and 6.88 ± 0.61 mm, respectively, and those produced by the Mg-CDs were 24.92 ± 0.69 mm and 14.02 ± 0.86 mm, respectively. These results showed that both the Mg-CDs and CDs could effectively inhibit gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria. The bacteriostatic effect of the Mg-CDs was greater than that of the CDs, and the bacteriostatic activity of both materials against S. aureus was greater than that against E. coli. A possible reason for these results is that Mg-CDs are more prone to participating in electrostatic interactions with the monolayer peptidoglycans, proteins and phospholipids of gram-positive bacteria, which leads to the breakdown of bacterial biofilms and release of their intracellular components, whereas the thicker bilayer peptidoglycan cell walls of gram-negative bacteria make it harder for the Mg-CDs to establish these interactions (Riahi et al., 2024). With respect to the antibacterial effect of the CDs, previous studies reported that ultrasmall CDs can effortlessly pass through the cell walls of microorganisms to interfere with cellular activities and induce reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation via oxygen molecule transfer. This results in the formation of hydroxyl radicals (OH•) and singlet oxygen, which may cause oxidative stress, disrupted cellular homeostasis, and impaired cellular processes (Riahi et al., 2024). Additionally, the Mg2+ ions released by the Mg-CDs can interact with the cytoplasmic substances in bacterial cells to generate ROS, damaging the bacterial cell wall and inducing cell lysis (Khan et al., 2024).

Fig. 2 shows the surfaces and cross-sections of the films. No pores or cracks were observed on the surface of C-P/GG. The addition of Mg-CDs did not damage the surface structure of the composite film, which was denser and smoother. The surface of the film with 7 wt% Mg-CDs was most uniform, which indicates that the Mg-CDs are evenly dispersed and are compatible with the film matrix. Sul et al. (2023) reported similar results for the surfaces of films after banana peel CDs were added to a chitosan/gelatin matrix. The cross-section of C-P/GG was rough, and the boundary between the chitosan and gellan gum layers was clearly seen. As the amount of added Mg-CDs increased, the roughness of the outer gellan layer decreased gradually, and the interface between the CS and gellan gum became more uniform and denser. There are two reasons for this phenomenon. (Ⅰ) Small nanoparticles occupy the pores in a polymer film matrix well, which improves the film density (Jiang et al., 2021). (II) Due to the hydrogen bonding interactions between the Mg-CDs and the film matrix, a dense three-dimensional network structure was formed, which increased interfacial adhesion. The enhanced barrier and mechanical properties that resulted from the dense structure expand the range of applications for these films (Chen et al., 2022). EDS elemental mapping analysis of the C-P/G-7%CD film is shown in Fig. 2c. The gellan gum polymer backbone consists of carbon and oxygen. Moreover, Mg peaks were observed in the EDS spectrum, verifying the successful doping of Mg-NPs into the polymer matrix. In addition, elemental mapping analysis of the C-P/G-7%CD film revealed that the individual elements were evenly distributed throughout the surface, indicating that the Mg-CDs were well distributed throughout the film matrix.

The functional groups on the surface of the films were examined by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). The wide absorption bands centered at 3200–3500 cm−1 in the film spectrum corresponded to -OH stretching and overlap with the vibrational NH stretching band (Roy et al., 2020). The peak at approximately 2930 cm−1 corresponded to tensile vibrations of -CH2 or -C-H groups. The characteristic absorption peak near 1635 cm−1 was attributed to CO groups, the peak near 1411 cm−1 was attributed to the tensile vibrations of C–N groups, and the peaks near 1155 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 were attributed to the tensile vibrations of C–O groups (Wang et al., 2023b). The peak at 3373.99 cm−1 in the C-P/GG spectrum shifted to 3373.46 cm−1 (C-P/G-1%CD), 3370.10 cm−1 (C-P/G-3%CD), 3369.86 cm−1 (C-P/G-5%CD), and 3362.26 cm−1 (C-P/G-7%CD) with increasing addition of Mg-CDs. These results indicated that the Mg-CDs had formed hydrogen bonds with the film matrix. The spectra of all the films were similar to that of C-P/GG, with no effect due to the chemical composition of the polymer chain apart from slight alterations in the peak positions due to the incorporation of the Mg-CDs. Thus, the uniform binding of the Mg-CDs to the film matrix and their good compatibility were confirmed.

The XRD results (Fig. 2e) of the film matrix without Mg-CDs displayed peaks at 8.06°, 11.34°, and 18.52°, indicating the amorphous phase of chitosan (Zhang et al., 2021), whereas the diffraction peak at 2θ = ∼ 22.66° is typical of gellan gum due to its double helix crystal structure (Zhai et al., 2020). The intensities of the diffraction peaks slightly decreased as the Mg-CD content increased. This may have occurred because the interaction between the Mg-CDs and the film matrix attenuated the interactions between the original polymers. However, when the Mg-CD content was 7%, the crystallinity of the film had improved tremendously due to the enhanced interactions between the Mg-CDs and the film matrix. Additionally, the polymer molecular structure became more ordered, which improved the physical properties of the films.

The thermal stabilities of the film samples were evaluated by TGA, and the results are presented in Fig. 2f. The film samples showed 3 stages of mass loss. The first thermal decomposition was observed at approximately 30–120 °C due to the evaporation of physically adsorbed water. The second peak started at 120 °C and ended at 280 °C and was mainly due to the thermal degradation of amino and hydroxymethyl groups. The third thermal degradation occurred at 280–600 °C and was caused by the destruction of the film matrix and the biomolecular aromatic rings in PCA (Wang et al., 2023a). Compared with those of C-P/GG, the rates of the second and third decomposition steps of the C-P/GG-CD films were slightly lower. When heating was terminated (600 °C), the retention of dry matter decreased in the following order: C-P/G-7%CD (29.68%) > C-P/G-5%CD (29.16%) > C-P/G-3%CD (28.42%) > C-P/G-1%CD (27.84%) > C-P/GG (26.87%). The thermal stability of the films containing Mg-CDs was greater than that of C-P/GG, which was attributed to the presence of hydrogen bonds between the -OH groups of the Mg-CDs and the -OH groups of the polymer chains, which enhanced the thermal properties of the composite films. Sul et al. (2023) reported a similar phenomenon in which the addition of carbon dots improved the thermal stability of the gelatin/chitosan films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, moisture content (MC) and water solubility (WS)
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 shows the thickness, MC, and WS values of the films. The thickness of the C-P/GG film was 0.131 ± 0.08 mm. The addition of Mg-CDs significantly increased the film thickness (p < 0.05). This occurred because an increase in the solid content led to an increase in the film thickness. C-P/GG had the highest MC (20.88 ± 0.44%) among all the film samples. With the addition of Mg-CDs, the MC of the film samples gradually decreased because of the formation of hydrogen bonds between the Mg-CDs and the polymer matrix, which weakened the interactions between the polymer and water molecules. The WS of food packaging reflects the water resistance of films. After the Mg-CDs were added to the C-P/GG film, the WS significantly decreased (p < 0.05). This was mainly because the distribution of the Mg-CDs in the composite film was relatively uniform, resulting in the formation of hydrogen bonds between the Mg-CDs and the biopolymer, which effectively reduced the number of free hydrophilic groups.

The mechanical properties of the Mg-CD-incorporated films were determined by measuring the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) ( Fig. 3a-b). The TS of the film samples tremendously (p < 0.05) increased with increasing Mg-CD content. This was a result of the formation of hydrogen bonds between the Mg-CDs and the film matrix, which generated a dense three-dimensional mesh structure. Similarly, The TS of film samples increased greatly when green tea CDs were added to chitosan/gelatin matrix (Khan et al., 2023). In addition, the SEM images revealed that the film samples became compact and smooth after the Mg-CDs were added. This smooth and dense structure facilitated the improvement in the TS of the film samples. When the Mg-CD content was 1%, the EAB of the films increased substantially (p < 0.05). However, as the content of Mg-CDs continued to increase, as with the C-P/G-3%CD, C-P/G-5%CD, and C-P/G-7%CD films, the EAB decreased significantly (p < 0.05) but remained greater than that of the C-P/GG film. The addition of more than a certain amount of nanofillers along with an increase in the mechanical strength increases the rigidity of the composite structure, which could cause this decrease in the EAB (Khan et al., 2023). A similar phenomenon was reported by Priyadarshi et al. (2021), in which the EAB of gelatin/agar-based films first increased but then decreased with increasing amounts of sulfur quantum dots.

WVP is an important parameter to indicate the quality of biopolymer films, and as less water moves from the food to the environment around it, the quality of the film increases. As shown in Fig. 3c, the WVP value of C-P/GG was the highest, which showed that C-P/GG had poor water vapor barrier performance. The addition of Mg-CDs caused a significant decrease in the WVP of the composite films (p < 0.05), and C-P/G-7%CD had the lowest WVP value (3.53 ± 0.14). This can be attributed to the addition of nanofillers that can occupy the space between the chains, forming a dense network structure and adding weaving pathways. This can enhance the effectiveness of the film as a water vapor barrier by preventing water vapor diffusion. In addition, the hydrogen bonds that formed in the polymer matrix can also improve the ability of the films to act as a water vapor barrier. These findings are similar to those of previous studies in which silver nanoparticles improved the barrier properties of starch/agar-based films (Roy & Rhim, 2022). The oxygen barrier capacity has a direct effect on the expiration date of food, and OP is a critical indicator for evaluating the oxygen barrier ability of films (Wang et al., 2023a). Fig. 3d shows the OP results for films. C-P/GG had the highest OP value (0.253 ± 0.031 cm3 mm m−2 day−1 atm−1), suggesting poor oxygen barrier performance. With the addition of Mg-CDs, the OP of the films decreased tremendously (p < 0.05). When the content of the Mg-CDs was 7 wt% (C-P/G-7%CD), the OP value was the lowest (0.053 ± 0.015 cm3 mm m−2 day−1 atm−1). Because the addition of Mg-CDs increases the density and stability of the internal structure of the double-layer films, the path that oxygen must take through the double-layer film becomes complicated. In addition, the barrier properties of Mg-CDs can be enhanced when hydrogen bonds are formed with the polymer groups. The addition of Mg-CDs caused the internal structure of the bilayer film to become denser and more stable, making the path of oxygen passage through the bilayer film more complicated. Moreover, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the Mg-CDs and polymer groups can strengthen the barrier performance of the films.

The WCA indicates the hydrophilic properties of a composite film surface. The larger the angle is, the poorer the wettability and the stronger the hydrophobicity of the material. Fig. 3e shows the WCA results. The WCA of C-P/GG was 64.48°, indicating that this surface is hydrophilic. With the addition of Mg-CDs, the WCAs of the films increased significantly (p < 0.05). The increase in the WCA may be due to the relatively uniform distribution of Mg-CDs nanoparticles in film. This arrangement facilitates the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the active functional groups on the surface of the polymer matrix and reduces the number of hydrophilic groups on the film surface (Roy & Rhim, 2022). The WCAs of the films containing Mg-CDs were all above 70°, suggesting their low hydrophilicity. As a result, the C-P/G-CD composite films displayed good moisture resistance and could be utilized in humid environments.

The color of food packaging films has a great influence on the appearance of the product and consumer preferences. A composite film was placed on a school logo, and the photographs are shown in Fig. 3f. The C-P/GG film matrix was light purple, whereas the double-layer films containing Mg-CDs were yellow. The color of the composite films deepened as the Mg-CD content increased. Additionally, the uniform color of the composite film further indicated the uniform distribution of Mg-CDs in the biopolymer matrix, as the Mg-CDs were slightly yellow. The physical appearance of the film was a result of its variation in color parameters. The color parameters of the films are detailed in Table 1. Upon Mg-CDs addition, the L* of composite films gradually decreased from 66.18 ± 0.54 to 45.78 ± 0.29, indicating a decrease in the brightness. Moreover, significant changes in the a* and b* values were found after adding of the Mg-CDs (p < 0.05). The a* values of the composite films increased from −4.10 ± 0.51 to 2.24 ± 0.09, and the b* values increased from −3.10 ± 0.90 to 16.89 ± 0.31, indicating that the color changed to yellow after the Mg-CDs were added. The improved a* and b* values resulted from combined effect of color of PCA and Mg-CDs (Riahi et al., 2024). ΔE represents the total color difference. Relative to that of the C-P/GG film, the ΔE values of the composite films supplemented with Mg-CDs increased tremendously (p < 0.05). On the one hand, anthocyanins cause color changes in films, but on the other hand, the color changes are caused primarily by the selective absorption of light by the doped CDs (Riahi et al., 2024).

The transmittance of the films from 200–800 nm is shown in Fig. 3g. The C-P/GG film displayed high visible light transmittance (400 nm or more) and high film transparency but displayed low light transmittance under UV light below 400 nm, particularly between 200–320 nm, showing certain UV blocking properties due to the abilities of CS and anthocyanins to block UV radiation. As the Mg-CD content increased, the light transmittance of the films clearly decreased. When the Mg-CDs content was 7 wt%, the UV-B transmittance (280–320 nm) was almost completely blocked. These results indicated that C-P/G-CD films with good UV-blocking properties can protect packaged products from UV-induced photooxidation and photodegradation.

The DPPH radical scavenging activities of the films, which reflect the antioxidant capacities of the films, were determined, and the results are detailed in Fig. S2a (Supplementary Information). The DPPH radical scavenging capacity of C-P/GG was 21.60 ± 1.11%. This was attributed to the fact that anthocyanins can transfer H atoms from their own hydroxyl groups to eliminate free radicals. With the addition of Mg-CDs, the DPPH radical scavenging capacity of the films substantially (p < 0.05) improved. In particular, the C-P/G-7%CD film quenched all of the DPPH radicals. The enhanced antioxidant capacity of the composite film with Mg-CDs was due to the functional groups located on the surface of the Mg-CDs that can provide hydrogen atoms. These hydrogen atoms can react with fatty acid free radicals, interrupt free radical chain reactions and prevent the automatic oxidation of products (Riahi et al., 2024). Therefore, the composite films containing Mg-CDs had good antioxidant activity and were suitable for use as active packaging materials.

Fig. S2b-c (Supplementary Information) shows the antibacterial activities of the films. The antibacterial activity of C-P/GG was the weakest. As the Mg-CD content increased, the antibacterial activity of the composite film increased substantially (p < 0.05). The significantly enhanced antibacterial ability of the composite films was due to the antibacterial effects of the Mg-CDs. The buildup of Mg-CDs in bacterial cells hinders cell wall permeability; additionally, Mg-CDs can also interact with fatty acid chains in the lipid bilayer, interrupting the membrane structure and causing bacterial death. Moreover, the active PCA can interact with intracellular bacterial proteins to limit bacterial reproduction and development (Dong et al. 2023). In particular, the lower bactericidal rate of the films against E. coli could be because of the presence of lipopolysaccharide in the E. coli cell membranes and walls, which prevents the infiltration of active Mg-CDs and PCA. Riahi et al. (2024) showed that anthocyanins combined with carrageenan-based films and Ti-doped carbon dots extracted from sweet potato skins had significant antibacterial effects.

3.3.10. pH-response ability and NH
3
-sensing ability.

pH sensitivity is a fundamental characteristic of colorimetric packaging films that can be utilized to monitor the freshness of food products since food spoilage often occurs with alterations in pH (Jiang et al. 2023). The color changes of the films immersed in various buffer solutions (pH 3.0 to 11.0) are detailed in Fig. 3h. The pattern of color change of the PCA solution was similar to that of the films. The film samples were red at pH 3.0. The films became light pink or reddish purple at pH values ranging from 4.0–6.0 and purple at pH 7.0. The C-P/G-CD films displayed deeper and more pronounced color transformations, which may be due to stronger electrostatic interactions between the functional group of Mg-CDs, polymer matrix and PCA. This improves the adsorption and stability of the anthocyanins in the polymer chain and thus enhances the discoloration of the film samples (Riahi et al., 2024). The film containing 7 wt% Mg-CDs exhibited different color changes, possibly because the color from the Mg-CDs masked the color of the anthocyanins, but the color shift of the C-P/G-7%CD film at different pH values was still significant and obvious (Riahi et al., 2024). These data demonstrated that C-P/G-CD films can be used as indicators of quality for monitoring pH-sensitive foods.

The sensitivity of a film to ammonia is a direct reflection of the film’s sensitivity to monitoring the spoilage of high-protein foods. The color change of the bilayer films in the presence of NH3 and the relative color difference (S values) are shown in Fig. 3i-j. The films displayed distinct color changes after 10 min, suggesting that the films were very sensitive to ammonia. Ammonia vapor can permeate the polymer matrix and become hydrated to form hydrated ammonia (NH3·H2O). The hydrated ammonia is later hydrolyzed, generating ammonium ions (NH4 +) and hydroxyl ions (OH−). The presence of NH4 + results in an alkaline environment on the film surface, generating β-amino acids and inducing the smart film to change to green–yellow (Cheng et al. 2022). The S values of all the film samples changed significantly, and a perceptible color change was observed. Hence, when monitoring packaged foods, C-P/G-CD films can be used to sensitively detect the stat of the food state.

It is essential that films be stable to light for real-world applications. In this work, the color stability of the films was tested under UV light and visible light. Visible light was produced by a white fluorescent lamp (∼ 400–760 nm). After storage, the color of the film samples changed, as shown in Fig. 4a. After 21 days of storage, there was a slight change in the color of all the composite films, and the C-P/GG film displayed the most obvious color change. The relative color change (S value) was used to quantify the color change of the film. Illumination affects the stability of anthocyanins, and prolonged exposure to light degrades and discolors the anthocyanins. In a previous report, Yang et al. (2021) developed a bilayer film in which gelatin-doped nanoparticles were used as the outer layer and gellan gum-doped mulberry anthocyanins were used as the inner layer. The relative color change (S values) before and after exposure under light conditions were used to indicate the stability of the film. From Fig. 4b, all colorimetric films displayed S values increased with radiation time, and S values decreased as the Mg-CDs concentration increased. The colors of the film samples after exposure to UV light (λ = 365 nm) are shown in Fig. 4d. The S value of the C-P/GG film was the highest (16.5% at 36 h) (Fig. 4e), indicating that the C-P/GG film was most vulnerable to UV light. The incorporation of Mg-CDs enhanced the light stability of the colorimetric films. At 36 h, the S values of the C-P/G-1%CD film, C-P/G-3%CD film, and C-P/G-5%CD film were 14.64%, 12.30% and 9.47%, respectively. When the Mg-CD content was 7 wt%, the S value was only 4.69%, which was much lower than that of the C-P/GG film. Compared with that of C-P/GG, the remarkably improved color stability of the C-P/G-CD films was due to the strong UV absorption of the Mg-CDs. Figs. 4c and 4f shows the FT-IR spectra of the films after 21 days of visible irradiation and 36 h of ultraviolet irradiation, respectively. After 21 d and 36 h of illumination, compared with the original FTIR spectra (Fig. 2d), the spectra displayed a new characteristic peak at approximately 2800 cm−1, which was attributed to -CH2- vibrations, indicating that illumination had an influence on chemical structure of the film. As the Mg-CD content increased, the intensities of the characteristic peaks slowly decreased, indicating that the addition of Mg-CDs could enhance the light stability of the composite film.

In this work, the biological safety of the developed film was proven by observing the effects of a C-P/G-7%CD aqueous solution on the development of hydroponic bean sprout seeds (Qin et al. 2023). A detailed analysis of the biosafety evaluation is provided in the Supporting Information.

Fresh pork is easily infected by microorganisms in the air. These microorganisms multiply on the pork surface to produce TVB-N, changing the pH value of the pork and leading to a change in the color of the intelligent pork packaging (Wang et al., 2023a). C-P/G-7%CD was selected for pork freshness monitoring because of its superior performance overall ( Fig. 5). As shown in Fig. 5c, the initial pH and TVB-N value of the fresh pork at room temperature were 6.57 and 7.65 mg/100 g, respectively. On Day 1, the color of the C-P/G-7%CD film changed to yellowgreen, the pH of the pork was 7.30, and the TVB-N reached 15.57 mg/100 g. The above results were due to the large number of microorganisms on the pork surface, which breakdown proteins to generate TVB-N, leading to alterations in the pH (Zhang et al., 2022). According to the National Standard of the People’s Republic of China (2016) (China, 2016) GB 2707–2016 (Hygienic standard for fresh (frozen) meat of livestock), the acceptable TVB-N content in fresh meat should not exceed 15 mg/100 g. On Day 1, pork TVB-N values were greater than 15 mg/100 g, at which point it should not be consumed by consumers; additionally, the film S value increased to 7.5%, corresponding to a ΔE > 5 (Table S1 in Supporting Information), and a significant color change in the film could be observed by the naked eye (Yun, Chen, Zhang, & Dong, 2023). On Day 3, the pork TVB-N content and pH significantly increased (p < 0.05) to 60.30 mg/100 g and 7.98, respectively, which was due to the increase in organic amine production by the bacteria growing on the surface of the pork. The weight loss and S value were 5.3% and 14.7%, respectively. At this point, the film was an obvious green color, indicating that the pork had been covered with a large number of microorganisms and was no longer suitable for consumption according to Chinese standards. The pork remained fresh longer at 4 °C than at room temperature. The TVB-N of pork exceeded 15 mg/100 g on Day 6 at 4 °C, at which point the S value was 8.9%. When the S value is greater than 8.9%, pork is inedible. These results indicate that C-P/G-7%CD can be utilized for monitoring pork freshness.

In this study, C-P/GG and C-P/G-CD bilayer colorimetric films were successfully prepared via layer-by-layer self-assembly. SEM and XRD analyses not only verified the successful preparation of the bilayer films but also proved that the film substrates were compatible with the Mg-CDs. When the amount of added Mg-CDs was 7 wt%, the overall performance of the film was the best, and the oxygen permeability, water vapor permeability, and tensile strength increased by 0.79-, 0.94-, and 3.18-fold, respectively. Additionally, the antioxidant capacity of the film was 91.77%, the size of the E. coli inhibition zone increased from 8.07 mm to 19.60 mm, and that of S. aureus increased from 9.43 mm to 22.17 mm. In addition, the color stability of the colorimetric films under visible and UV light improved with increasing Mg-CD content. The bilayer films containing Mg-CDs were also pH- and ammonia-sensitive. The C-P/G-7%CD films were used as smart indicators to monitor the change in pork freshness at room temperature and 4 °C. The color of the films changed from yellow to green and was obvious to the naked eye.

The film developed in this study has shown promising applications in meat food monitoring, and the potential of using this film to monitor the freshness and extend the shelf-life of other meat, fruit, vegetable, and grain products will be further investigated in the future. However, smart food packaging is currently mainly prepared on a small scale in the laboratory at a high cost. Ideally, smart packaging should be reusable and easily recyclable. Therefore, expanding the practical applications of food packaging and developing safe and sustainable packaging materials are the focus of our future research. Moreover, cytotoxicity and feeding studies need to be performed to further evaluate the biosafety of the developed films, which will be the focus of our future research.
